Title: SERVER Alert - Critical - ORX-DC-AD-03.isi.local: (10.100.106.51) - Disk Utilization is 100%, threshold value for this monitor is 90%
Notes: As per the alert connected to ORX-DC-AD-03 download and install TreeSize, a disk space management tool that provides a detailed overview of the disk usage across folders and files on the system. After installation, TreeSize was run to analyse the disk. TreeSize scans the system and generates a report showing the size of all folders and files in a hierarchical structure so carefully review all folders and files occupying excessive space. Based on the review, we proceeded to clear unnecessary files that were identified as no longer needed. This included: Temporary Files: Files in the system's temporary folder are often created by applications during installation, updates, or other processes; clear these temporary files to free up space. Recycle Bin: emptied the Recycle Bin, where deleted files are temporarily stored. This ensured that deleted files were permanently removed from the disk and no longer took up space. Downloaded Items: System error memory dump files, Old Windows updates, previous Windows installations other system cache files. Now Sufficient Free Space Available.

Title: ORX : Learning and Knowledge Transfer
Notes:
Understanding Schedular Code

  • The scheduler retrieves Patient Info from the OrthoXpressDB tables using multiple joins.

  • After fetching the data, it excludes records that are already present in the OXPLiveNg table.

  • Only records that do not exist in OXPLiveNg are selected for insertion.

  • The same process is applied to Jobs, Claims, and Claim Services:

    • Data is fetched from the relevant source tables.

    • Existing records in OXPLiveNg are excluded.

    • Only the new records are inserted into OXPLiveNg.

  • The remaining parts of the scheduler code will be studied tomorrow.

Title: ORX: Learning
Notes:
I reviewed the AR summary data, which is categorized by age group and payer group, with age ranges divided as follows: 0-30, 30-60, 60-90, 90-120, and 120+.
Then, I checked the Claim History report, which provides the minimum, maximum, and average values grouped by payer.

Title: ORX : Learning and Knowledge Transfer
Notes:
• While reviewing the implementation, I observed that the AR Summary data is being displayed directly from the ARSummary table in the database rather than being calculated dynamically at runtime.

  • The table contains details grouped by Payer Group, and this grouped data is directly used for displaying the AR Summary.

  • In addition to the total amounts, the system is also showing the percentage values for each aging bucket.

  • These percentages are calculated based on the amount in each bucket divided by the total AR amount, and importantly, these calculated percentage values are already stored in the ARSummary table instead of being computed on the fly.

  • This suggests that the AR Summary data, including bucket-wise amounts and percentages, is likely pre-calculated and persisted in the database, possibly through a background process or maybe in OrthoXpressDB for performance optimization and faster reporting.

  • Additionally, I noticed that in tables such as PatientFiles and PatientInsurance, the data appears to be inserted or updated by the Scheduler process.

  • In both tables, there is a column named CreatedBy, and all records, the value is set to “Scheduler.”

  • This indicates that these records are being created automatically through a scheduled background job or sync process, rather than being manually entered by a user.

Title: Wifi Issue
Notes: An issue with the Wi-Fi connection at ORX Riverside was reported by Brett via the Teams channel. I performed initial troubleshooting and confirmed that everything appeared to be operating normally on the Meraki side. Further investigation showed that the issue was isolated to a specific SSID. Upon reviewing the configuration, I discovered that the bandwidth limit had been set to 500 kb/s. After receiving confirmation from Brett regarding the appropriate allocation, I updated the bandwidth limit to 50 Mbps per guest user. Additionally, I identified that the Meraki firmware is due for an update. Since applying the update may cause downtime, it will be scheduled for after-hours

Title: Wifi status in Riverside, please check the status of the wifi connection. Showing extremely slow connection
Notes: Following Sai’s handover to perform the Riverside AP firmware upgrade after business hours, I scheduled and executed the upgrade during the maintenance window on Feb 17, 2026, and confirmed completion. Post-upgrade, I validated AP health and cloud connectivity, then reviewed the event logs, which showed multiple client disconnects, “client not heard” messages, and channel changes consistent with RF issues rather than firmware faults. I analyzed client usage and the RF Spectrum, which indicated high 2.4 GHz channel utilization and significant packet loss on both bands (worse on 2.4 GHz), confirming interference and congestion as the primary cause of slow Wi-Fi.
